title: Project Nexus — Technical Specification and Constraints
text:
Project Nexus is a data platform modernization initiative with a fixed total budget of $847,000, which must not be exceeded under any circumstances. The budget is divided across five areas: Module A integration work is allocated $12,400; Module B development receives $23,800; infrastructure provisioning is capped at $38,500; quality assurance and testing has a budget of $9,200; and a contingency reserve of $4,100 is held for unforeseen expenditures.

The project operates on a five-milestone schedule. The design review must be completed by February 14th. The working prototype is due March 29th. System integration is scheduled for April 17th. The beta release must ship by May 31st. The final production release is targeted for July 8th.

Five individuals hold named responsibilities. Dr. Elena Vasquez serves as lead architect and has final authority over all technical decisions. Marcus Okonkwo is the QA lead and owns the test plan and acceptance criteria. Nadia Hoffman is the project manager responsible for stakeholder communication and timeline governance. Ravi Krishnaswamy is the DevOps engineer responsible for CI/CD pipelines and deployment automation. Sofia Delacroix is the security officer who must sign off on all data handling procedures before the beta release.

The technical stack is fully locked. The primary database is PostgreSQL 15, and no other database engine may be introduced without written approval from Dr. Elena Vasquez. The application runtime is Python 3.12; mixing language versions within the codebase is prohibited. All services must be containerized using Docker 26.1. The continuous integration pipeline runs exclusively on GitHub Actions; migrating to another CI provider requires a formal change request. The distributed caching layer must use Redis 8.0, and the version must not be downgraded.

Additional operational constraints: the system must maintain 99.97% uptime after the production release. Each pull request requires approval from a minimum of three reviewers before merging. The maximum permitted API response latency at the 99th percentile is 180 milliseconds. All data must be retained for a minimum of ten years in compliance with regulatory requirements.
